That is why working capital control is so important: it ensures the financial statements reflect economic reality rather than habit or hope. This discipline supports faithful representation and helps protect the organization from both misstatement and avoidable loss. It also provides a clear accounting basis for management action, which is especially important when balances sit unresolved across multiple periods.

Operational Execution and Examples
The quality of this control area is visible in the small things: whether invoices are complete, reminders are logged, reconciliations are current, provisions are reasoned, write-offs are approved, and disputes are owned. When those small things are done well, the ledger becomes trustworthy. When they are done badly, the organization pays through lost cash, slow reporting, and avoidable audit findings. The finance function should therefore treat this area as a living operating system for the balance sheet, not as a set of isolated reconciliations. Every good process here contributes directly to liquidity, accountability, and confidence. Over time, the organization should aim to make the routine so disciplined that exceptions stand out immediately instead of hiding inside the normal flow.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
Ultimately, managing debtors, creditors, and bad debts is an act of stewardship. It is about protecting assets, meeting obligations, and giving transparent information to those who rely on the organization. By embedding strong controls, using disciplined reviews, and keeping the file evidence-based, the organization reduces the chance of hidden risk and increases the quality of decision-making. This is one of the clearest examples of how finance supports governance in daily practice. It demonstrates that the ledger is not merely a historical record. It is a managed reflection of real economic commitments and claims that require continuous attention.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Effective working capital management is a strategic imperative because it directly affects liquidity, profitability, risk, and resilience. The ledger is not only a record of the past; it is the foundation for future planning. By mastering these controls, the organization improves its ability to respond, invest, and sustain operations. The result is a finance function that supports mission delivery with discipline rather than merely recording the consequences of weak control. In strategic terms, working capital management is one of the easiest ways to see whether the organization is merely accounting for transactions or actually managing them responsibly.

Send to the team:
- “Please remember that every ledger balance represents a real economic obligation or claim; manage them with discipline, transparency, and stewardship.”
Key point:
- Working capital management is a strategic imperative and an act of stewardship that protects the organization’s future.
Official links:
- [IFRS Foundation: IFRS 9 Financial Instruments](https://www.ifrs.org/issued-standards/list-of-standards/ifrs-9-financial-instruments/)
- [IAS 1: Presentation of Financial Statements](https://www.ifrs.org/issued-standards/list-of-standards/ias-1-presentation-of-financial-statements/)
- [COSO: Internal Control – Integrated Framework](https://www.coso.org/guidance-on-ic)
Rows: 30
Table 10
Q & A Bank Accounts and Banking Controls
1. Q: Why are bank accounts and banking controls critical to organizational finance?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Bank accounts are one of the most important control points in any organization because they sit at the intersection of cash management, operational continuity, fiduciary responsibility, and financial reporting. Almost every meaningful cash flow eventually passes through a bank account, whether it involves salaries, supplier payments, donor receipts, tax remittances, refunds, intercompany transfers, or temporary holding balances. That makes the integrity of bank accounts central to the integrity of the finance function itself. If a bank account is weakly governed, the weakness is not isolated. It can affect the general ledger, cash forecasting, payment approval, donor compliance, and the organization’s ability to continue operating without interruption.

Governance and Control Framework
Strong banking controls are not limited to theft prevention. They also protect against duplicate payments, unauthorized changes to signatories, hidden accounts, stale reconciliations, weak access management, unsupported transfers, and poor audit trails. A proper banking framework treats each account as part of a life cycle: opened for a documented purpose, used only within approved authority, reconciled regularly, reviewed for continued need, and eventually closed when no longer required. This life-cycle approach matters because cash is the most liquid and therefore the most vulnerable asset. The organization must always know who can move it, why it is being moved, and whether the movement is legitimate. Where this discipline is absent, even small weaknesses can accumulate into major financial and reputational damage.

Operational Execution and Examples
A small control lapse can become a large loss if it is ignored. For example, an unsigned account-opening request may lead to an account being used before approvals are fully in place. A missing bank statement may conceal unauthorized withdrawals. A forgotten access right may allow a former employee to still view or initiate payments. Banking control failures often grow gradually, not suddenly. That is why organizations frequently discover the real problem only after losses, errors, or audit findings appear. The danger is less the single exception than the habit of accepting exceptions until they become normal practice. The bank account is often the first place where weak governance becomes visible in cash form.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The bank register, mandate file, access list, and reconciliation history should together prove that each account is complete, authorized, and continuously monitored. Auditors test whether the bank register matches the ledger, whether the signatories are current, whether there is evidence of independent review, and whether unusual items are followed up promptly. If these records are incomplete or out of date, the organization may be exposed to liquidity crises, covenant breaches, fraud exposure, and reputational damage. In large or multi-country organizations, the risk multiplies because cash may be dispersed across institutions and currencies, making poor control harder to detect and more costly to repair. The stronger the organization’s need for cash reliability, the stronger the banking control structure must be.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Banking controls are also an enterprise risk management issue. They affect treasury planning, business continuity, sanctions exposure, AML risk, cross-border cash movement, and the organization’s ability to maintain donor or investor confidence. A bank account is not just a payment channel; it is a strategic operating asset. When it is properly governed, management can rely on it for accurate cash position reporting and efficient liquidity management. When it is weakly governed, even good strategy can be undermined by poor execution at the cash level. Effective banking control therefore protects both day-to-day operations and long-term organizational resilience.

Send to the team:
- “Please treat every bank account as a critical control point requiring full life-cycle governance.”
Key point:
- Banking controls are the foundation of cash integrity and operational continuity.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
2. Q: What is the comprehensive scope of banking controls?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
The scope of banking controls must include every account, product, platform, and payment relationship through which the organization can hold, move, or risk cash. That means not only the obvious operating bank accounts, but also payroll accounts, savings accounts, foreign currency accounts, project accounts, receipt accounts, merchant accounts, escrow accounts, virtual IBANs, payment gateway balances, card settlement accounts, and any digital or custodial wallet used for organizational purposes. If the organization benefits from an account or is financially affected by it, then the account belongs inside the control framework. A narrow scope creates blind spots, and blind spots are where cash risk grows. The control perimeter should follow the money wherever the money can move.

Governance and Control Framework
The scope must also extend to banking products and services that create exposure even when they do not look like ordinary accounts. These include overdrafts, revolving credit lines, letters of credit, guarantees, direct debit authorities, standing orders, sweep arrangements, card programmes, and treasury products. Regulatory scope matters too. The organization must take into account Know Your Customer procedures, anti-money laundering rules, sanctions screening, tax reporting obligations such as FATCA and CRS where relevant, and local banking regulations. An account is never “just local” if it sits inside a wider legal and compliance environment. The control perimeter should therefore include both money movement and legal exposure, because the same weak point can create both financial and regulatory problems.

Operational Execution and Examples
Weakness often begins in the accounts people consider too small or too temporary to matter. A dormant account, a project-specific account, or a low-volume receipt account may remain unchecked for months and quietly become the most vulnerable point. The same is true for payment platforms and virtual accounts that are used only occasionally. If the organization only reviews the main current account, it may miss the real exposure elsewhere. A complete scope requires the central register to reflect every account, every product, every authorized user, and every relationship that can move money or create financial liability. This prevents surprise losses and makes the control environment visibly complete rather than selectively complete.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
A complete scope helps auditors verify that all special-purpose accounts are captured, tracked, reconciled, and reviewed. It also helps management demonstrate that no illicit financial flow is being facilitated through an unmonitored channel. The organization should be able to show that every account has an owner, a purpose, a control file, and a review schedule. If the scope is incomplete, control cannot be reliable because the review is only partial. In practical terms, the scope of banking controls should match the actual map of money movement across the organization, not the map of what was easiest to record last year. That is the difference between control design and control appearance.

Strategic Management and Reporting
A broad but disciplined scope supports treasury resilience. It allows leadership to understand where funds are held, which banks carry concentration risk, which accounts are active, and which relationships deserve renegotiation or consolidation. Strategic oversight begins with full visibility. If a bank account exists anywhere in the organization’s footprint, leadership should know why it exists, who uses it, what it supports, and what risk it introduces. That is what turns scope from a compliance checklist into a real risk-management tool. Proper scope also improves decision-making because treasury can plan liquidity from a complete map rather than from fragments of the cash structure.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure every account and banking product, regardless of size, is included in the central register.”
Key point:
- Scope must cover all accounts, products, transaction chains, and regulatory perimeters.
Official links:
- Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN): AML Regulations
- Financial Action Task Force (FATF)
- IFAC: Knowledge Gateway
3. Q: What are the core principles and control objectives for banking?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Banking controls should rest on a small set of durable principles that never stop being relevant: no account without approval, no transaction without support, no single person controlling the full transaction chain, and no balance left unreconciled without explanation. These principles are simple, but they are powerful because they apply whether banking is paper-based, online, local, global, manual, or highly automated. The objective is not merely to stop fraud. It is to make cash activity understandable, authorized, reviewable, and recoverable if something goes wrong. In a healthy control environment, cash flow is never treated as mysterious; it is always capable of explanation.

Governance and Control Framework
These principles support several control objectives. First, they protect funds from unauthorized movement. Second, they improve the accuracy of the accounting records. Third, they produce an audit trail that can be defended under review. Fourth, they help detect unusual activity before it becomes material. Fifth, they support treasury planning and liquidity forecasting. In a mature control environment, the principles are not hidden in policy documents. They are visible in day-to-day work, in who can approve, who can release, who can reconcile, and who can challenge unusual items. The simpler the process, the more likely staff can actually follow it consistently under pressure.

Operational Execution and Examples
A strong banking framework is one people can sustain. A policy that requires ten steps but is ignored in practice is weaker than a disciplined three-step process that everyone actually uses. Control quality is measured by consistency, not decoration. If staff understand the purpose of the review, the reason for the approval chain, and the consequence of skipping a step, they are more likely to comply. That makes control part of culture, not just procedure. It also means training matters: people need to know not just what to do, but why they are doing it and what risk they are preventing. Control that is understood is control that is more likely to survive staff turnover.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The principles must be supported by evidence. Every approval should be documented. Every bank statement should be retained. Every reconciliation should show preparer, reviewer, date, and resolution of differences. In modern systems, this increasingly includes digital signatures, access logs, online banking audit trails, and ERP integrations. These records matter because they prove that the control was not merely intended; it was actually performed. Internal and external auditors will examine both the policy and the evidence of execution. If the two do not match, the control is not effective. A control objective without evidence is just an assumption about behavior.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Clarity and repetition are more important than complexity. A bank control that everyone can understand and routinely execute is better than a highly sophisticated one that only specialists can sustain. The strategic objective is dependable behavior, because dependable behavior is what turns raw bank data into trusted cash information. When the banking framework is clear, management can use the data confidently for planning, forecasting, and decision-making without second-guessing whether the underlying numbers are reliable. The organization should therefore design banking control for reliability first and sophistication second.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure every control activity is documented, repeatable, and aligned with our core principles.”
Key point:
- Control quality is measured by consistency in practice, not elegance on paper.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
4. Q: How is governance and accountability structured for bank accounts?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Banking governance should assign clear accountability at multiple levels. Senior leadership and the Board approve banking policy and risk appetite. Finance leadership owns the framework. Treasury manages liquidity and banking relationships. Operational managers initiate transactions within approved limits. Independent review checks that the process is being followed. The key point is that responsibility must be named, visible, and understood. If no one clearly owns the account, everyone assumes someone else does, and weak control survives in the gap. Governance is therefore not only about authority; it is about clarity of ownership.

Governance and Control Framework
Every account should have a named owner. Every reconciliation should have a preparer and a reviewer. Every signatory should be formally approved. Governance means supervision as well as assignment. The person who prepares a payment should not be the only person who sees it, and the person who approves online banking access should not be able to hide those changes from management. This is why the governance structure should reflect the real chain of authority rather than a loose list of names. In well-governed systems, accountability does not disappear when the staff member changes; it remains tied to the role, the policy, and the documented process. This creates continuity, which is essential when staff turnover occurs.

Operational Execution and Examples
Clear ownership reduces confusion during exceptions. If a bank asks for updated mandates, everyone should know which role is responsible. If a statement is missing, everyone should know who chases it. If a reconciliation has an unresolved item, everyone should know who must investigate. Unclear ownership is one of the biggest reasons weak controls remain uncorrected for too long, because no one is certain who should act. A good governance framework eliminates that ambiguity. It also makes handover and staff turnover less dangerous because the next person can immediately see what they inherit and what remains outstanding. Control survives people when ownership is formalized.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The Board and Audit Committee are important because they set tone, oversight, and escalation expectations. Management must then implement practical controls that align with the policy. Internal audit independently tests whether those controls actually work. External auditors examine whether the governance structure is complete enough to support reliable reporting. The most effective governance arrangements make it easy to see who decided what, who checked it, and who is accountable when something goes wrong. That visibility is the difference between a formal structure and real governance. A paper hierarchy without operational follow-through is not accountability; it is decoration.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Strong accountability survives personnel change. It turns banking from a personal habit into an organizational discipline. That matters because strategic cash management depends on continuity. Leadership should always be able to answer: who owns each account, who reviews each balance, who can authorize each movement, and who escalates unresolved risk. When those answers are clear, the organization can trust the banking data as a foundation for cash planning and control. Governance is therefore the mechanism that transforms bank control from a local routine into an enterprise function.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure every bank account has a named owner and every reconciliation has an independent reviewer.”
Key point:
- Clear ownership and supervision are the bedrock of effective banking governance.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- IFAC: Knowledge Gateway
5. Q: What is the controlled process for opening a new bank account?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Opening a new bank account should always begin with a documented business case. The organization should explain why the account is needed, what activity it will support, why existing accounts are insufficient, and what risks the new account may introduce. That business justification matters because every additional account creates more work, more review, more access control, and more reconciliation. If the account is not necessary, it should not exist. If it is necessary, it should be created only through formal approval and documented configuration. Opening is therefore a governance decision, not a routine administrative event.

Governance and Control Framework
The selection of the bank should be deliberate. The organization should consider reliability, service quality, digital access, branch access, fee structure, foreign currency capability, payment speed, security, and compatibility with internal systems. The business case should include the proposed signatories, the intended use, account restrictions, and the approval route. Once approved, the account must be recorded immediately in the bank register, accounting system, and central file. No account should become active before the governance record is complete. That sequence matters because a bank account without a control file is already a control weakness. Formal opening discipline also discourages unnecessary duplication of accounts across departments or projects.

Operational Execution and Examples
The opening stage sets the standard for everything that follows. If the account is opened informally, it is more likely to be used informally. If access is vague, it is more likely to remain vague. If the purpose is not documented, staff may later use the account for unrelated transactions. A controlled opening process prevents duplicate accounts, unused accounts, and hidden accounts from multiplying quietly. It also prevents urgent requests from bypassing proper review. The more important the account, the more important it is that the opening record be complete before use begins. A strong opening record makes later review, audit, and closure much easier.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
After approval, the organization must ensure the bank account is correctly configured in the ERP, with the right ledger codes, bank codes, approval workflow, and reconciliation setup. If H2H integration or API connectivity is involved, the interface should be tested before go-live. Test transactions should confirm that data flows correctly and that security settings work as intended. Any mismatch between the bank, the accounting system, and the authority matrix should be corrected before the account is used operationally. This reduces the chance that a live payment will expose a configuration error. A well-documented opening file is one of the strongest signs that the account is under control from the start.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Each new account adds operational overhead and strategic complexity. By enforcing a strict opening process, the organization prevents account sprawl and preserves treasury visibility. Leadership can then manage fewer, better-controlled accounts instead of many loosely governed ones. This makes cash management easier, reporting cleaner, and risk more visible. Opening is not a routine formality; it is the point at which a new cash-control environment begins. The more disciplined the opening process, the more stable the organization’s long-term banking architecture becomes.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure every new account has a documented business case and formal approval before activation.”
Key point:
- Account opening is a controlled governance event, not a routine administrative formality.
Official links:
- Association for Financial Professionals: Treasury Management
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
6. Q: How should due diligence and bank selection be conducted?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Bank selection should be based on due diligence, not convenience alone. The organization should evaluate whether the bank is financially stable, operationally capable, digitally secure, and able to support the organization’s payment and reporting needs. The bank should also be judged on practical factors such as service quality, access to statements, cutoff times, customer support, branch reach, dispute resolution, and compatibility with internal finance systems. The point is to choose a banking partner that can be controlled well over time, not merely one that was easiest to open. Bank choice is a risk decision as much as an operating decision.

Governance and Control Framework
Selection criteria should be written and applied consistently. This often includes service charges, minimum balances, foreign currency capability, cash handling capability, online security features, payment rails, and the bank’s ability to support required reporting or integrations. If there is an approved banking panel, the choice should normally come from that list. If an exception is needed, the reason should be documented and approved by the appropriate authority. Due diligence should also include review of the bank’s terms and conditions, access recovery process, dormant account rules, and correspondent banking exposure. The organization should understand the rules before it commits to the relationship. What looks small at opening can become expensive later if due diligence is weak.

Operational Execution and Examples
A low-friction opening process is not enough if the bank becomes hard to govern later. An apparently convenient bank may have weak statement access, poor support, limited controls, or difficult cross-border settlement. The practical question is not just whether the account can be opened, but whether it can be managed safely and efficiently throughout its life. Due diligence should also assess geographical and political risk, especially where capital controls, currency restrictions, sanctions exposure, or correspondent banking risk may affect the organization’s ability to move funds. A poor choice can create problems long after the account is opened. Therefore, the selection process should look beyond the first transaction and consider the full control life cycle.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The organization should keep records of selection criteria, risk review, and the final decision. This creates accountability and helps explain why one bank was chosen over another. The bank’s own due diligence obligations also matter. KYC, AML, and sanctions expectations should be understood so that the organization can comply from the start rather than react after a problem. If the bank is exposed to higher-risk corridors or products, that should be reflected in the organization’s monitoring plan. Good due diligence is therefore both operational and compliance-related. It also gives auditors evidence that the choice was not arbitrary.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Selection should support resilience. If possible, the organization should avoid dependence on a single bank or jurisdiction for all critical cash needs. Diversification can reduce concentration risk and improve continuity if one provider becomes unavailable. The bank relationship should support the organization’s operating model, not hinder it. A strong banking partner contributes to strategic flexibility, reliable cash management, and smoother reporting, while a weak one adds hidden cost and risk. Good bank selection is therefore part of enterprise resilience, not merely procurement.

Send to the team:
- “Please conduct thorough due diligence on all banking partners, considering both operational fit and risk exposure.”
Key point:
- Bank selection must balance operational convenience with long-term control and risk management.
Official links:
- Association for Financial Professionals: Treasury Management
- Financial Action Task Force (FATF)
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
7. Q: How should bank mandates and authority limits be managed?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
The bank mandate is the formal instruction that tells the bank who may operate the account and how transactions must be authorized. It should match the organization’s internal delegation of authority. The mandate should state whether one signature is sufficient, whether dual authorization is required, whether certain payment types need special approval, and whether some individuals have limited roles. This document is a core control because the bank will rely on it when deciding whether to accept a payment instruction or a change request. If the mandate is unclear or outdated, the bank may follow the wrong authority.

Governance and Control Framework
Authority limits should be meaningful and practical. If limits are too high, they do not reduce risk; if they are too low, they create unnecessary bottlenecks and may encourage workarounds. The approval matrix should distinguish between routine low-risk payments and non-routine or high-value payments. The mandate should be reviewed periodically, not only when a person leaves. Roles change, teams change, and responsibility changes. A mandate that is not kept current can continue to authorize people who no longer have the right to act, which is a serious control gap. Governance should therefore combine authority, review, and documented change management.

Operational Execution and Examples
Changes to the mandate should be made through secure and controlled methods. The bank should verify identities, and the organization should keep written confirmation that the change was completed. If a bank still recognizes a former signatory, the account may be exposed even if the organization believes otherwise. A stale mandate is especially risky because it creates a false sense of security. It looks governed on paper while the actual authorization structure has already changed. That is why the mandate should be treated as a living governance document rather than a one-time form. Regular mandate review prevents this false comfort from developing.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The bank file should show current signatories, specimen signatures where relevant, restrictions, and approval history. The organization should also keep a record of who approved each mandate change and why. During audit, the question is not only whether a mandate exists, but whether it matches the current governance structure. If it does not, payments may have been authorized by people whose authority had already ended. Strong mandate control is therefore a protection against both fraud and administrative drift. It gives auditors confidence that the bank is aligned with policy, not just with old paperwork.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Clear mandates help preserve confidence in the payment environment. They ensure that authority is not assumed, but documented and current. When leadership can trust that the mandate reflects reality, the organization can move cash with confidence and without unnecessary delay. That confidence supports efficient disbursement, sound segregation of duties, and a stronger overall control environment. A current mandate structure is also easier to administer because the rules are visible and predictable.

Send to the team:
- “Please review and update all bank mandates annually to ensure they reflect current organizational authority.”
Key point:
- Bank mandates are living governance documents that must be regularly reviewed and securely updated.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
8. Q: What are the controls over signatories and electronic access rights?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
All signatories should be formally approved and documented, with the bank file showing names, roles, specimen signatures where relevant, and any restrictions on authority. Electronic banking should follow the same logic. Each user should have a unique login, a defined role, and a documented business need. Shared logins should be avoided because they destroy accountability. The banking platform should therefore reflect the real authority structure rather than a loose convenience structure. If the organization cannot say who performed a task, then the task was not properly controlled. Identity and authority must remain linked.

Governance and Control Framework
Maker-checker controls should be used wherever possible. The person who creates a payment should not release it, and the person who can release it should not be able to conceal that activity from management. User permissions should be no broader than needed. Access should be based on role and reviewed regularly. If someone transfers, resigns, or no longer needs banking access, it should be removed promptly. Delayed removal is a common control weakness because former access can remain active long after the job role has changed. The right to act in the banking system should end when the business need ends, not sometime later. Strong access discipline is one of the most important protections against misuse.

Operational Execution and Examples
Access control should also cover authentication devices, tokens, and emergency access arrangements. If an employee leaves, the token or device should be recovered immediately. If a token is missing, the account should be treated as at risk until the issue is resolved. Temporary or emergency access should be tightly logged and reviewed afterward. This is especially important in high-value payment environments where one compromised account can be used to move funds rapidly. A good access framework reduces both fraud risk and honest mistakes because the right people see only the functions they truly need. It also simplifies later investigation because activity can be tied back to one unique user.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
Access rights should be periodically reviewed against the HR roster and the authority matrix. Any mismatch should be corrected quickly. Auditors often look closely at banking access because it is a direct line to cash. They will test whether users are current, whether permissions are appropriate, and whether password or authentication controls are strong enough. If access is shared, excessive, or stale, the control environment is weaker even if no loss has yet occurred. Security is not just about blocking attacks; it is about proving accountability after the fact. Good logs and reviewed access records are therefore essential evidence of control.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Good access records reduce confusion and make it easier to see when the actual operating pattern no longer matches the approved structure. They also support strong fraud deterrence, because people are less likely to misuse a system when they know the activity is attributable to them personally. In strategic terms, controlled access protects cash, supports reporting quality, and keeps the banking environment aligned with current responsibilities rather than past assumptions. Access governance is therefore both a technology issue and a financial control issue.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure all electronic banking access rights are reviewed and aligned with current job responsibilities.”
Key point:
- Access rights must be unique, role-based, and promptly removed when no longer needed.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
9. Q: Why is segregation of duties critical in banking controls?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Segregation of duties is one of the most important banking controls because it prevents one person from controlling the entire transaction chain. The person who prepares a payment should not approve it, release it, and reconcile it afterward. When one individual can do all of those things, the risk of fraud and undetected error increases sharply. Segregation does not mean distrust; it means designing the process so that human error and human temptation are less likely to survive unnoticed. It is one of the simplest and strongest ways to protect cash. A sound process assumes that even honest people benefit from independent review.

Governance and Control Framework
Segregation should apply across the full bank process: receiving cash, recording transactions, preparing payments, approving payments, releasing payments, reconciling balances, and reviewing exceptions. In smaller organizations, complete separation may be difficult, but compensating controls should be introduced. Those can include independent management review, dual authorization, surprise checks, or limited emergency access with follow-up review. The objective is meaningful independence. A person should not be able to create, authorize, execute, and conceal the same transaction without an independent check somewhere in the chain. If that is possible, the process is not sufficiently controlled.

Operational Execution and Examples
Segregation also helps detect honest mistakes. If multiple people see different parts of the process, errors like duplicate payments, wrong beneficiaries, or incorrect amounts are more likely to be noticed before they become systemic. In practical terms, good banking control is easier to maintain when no one individual is trusted to do everything. The process itself carries part of the burden of honesty. That is why segregation is not a luxury of large organizations; it is a core control principle that should be adapted, not ignored, in smaller environments. Good design is better than heroic trust.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
Management override is a significant risk, especially where senior individuals can bypass normal controls. The organization should define emergency access carefully and monitor it closely. Frequent exceptions, manual releases, or repeated overrides should be investigated because they may point to a deeper control weakness. During audit, the question is whether the design and the actual behavior support independence. If a single person can do too much, the structure is weak even if the policy says otherwise. Therefore, segregation must be visible in practice, not only in the organization chart.

Strategic Management and Reporting
A control only exists if it works in practice. Segregation of duties protects assets, supports reporting reliability, and builds trust with donors, investors, and regulators. The organization’s cash discipline is stronger when no individual has unchecked control over the whole process. That is why segregation is one of the foundational principles of good banking governance and not just a technical detail in the approval workflow. It creates a system in which errors are harder to hide and misuse is harder to sustain.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure no single individual can initiate, approve, and reconcile the same banking transaction.”
Key point:
- Segregation of duties prevents one person from controlling the entire transaction chain.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- IFAC: Knowledge Gateway
10. Q: How should cheque stock and issuance be controlled?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Cheque stock should be treated as controlled inventory. It should be received, counted, stored securely, logged, and issued only to authorized staff. Blank cheques are not ordinary stationery; they are payment instruments and therefore need physical and procedural control. A lost, stolen, spoiled, or cancelled cheque should be recorded and handled according to policy. The organization should always be able to show how many cheques were received, where they are stored, who can access them, and which ones were used or voided. Cheque control is therefore a custody issue as much as a payment issue.

Governance and Control Framework
Every cheque should have a beneficiary, a purpose, and supporting documentation. Cheques payable to cash should generally be avoided unless there is a genuine operational need and a documented exception. Cheques should be crossed where relevant and matched against payment documentation before issue. The cheque register should show numbers, dates, amounts, payees, preparers, approvers, and final status. That record makes it possible to trace the instrument from custody to issuance. If the register is incomplete, the organization has lost visibility over a payment instrument that still has value. Like cash, cheques require both physical and documentary traceability.

Operational Execution and Examples
Lost or stolen cheques should be reported immediately so the bank can consider stop-payment action. Repeated cheque irregularities may indicate weak custody, poor reconciliation, or an attempt to bypass payment controls. Even when cheque use is declining, the control principle remains the same: physical payment instruments must be tracked carefully. If cheques are pre-signed, left unattended, or issued without supporting documents, the risk rises quickly. In those cases, the problem is usually not the cheque itself but the control attitude around it. The same discipline that protects cash should protect cheques.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The organization should consider services like positive pay or reverse positive pay where available. These services allow the bank and the organization to compare issued cheques with presented cheques to detect alterations or forgeries. Cheque stock counts should be periodic and independent. Any discrepancy should be investigated immediately. Auditors often treat cheque control as a signal of broader payment discipline because it reflects how carefully the organization handles other forms of cash movement as well. Weak cheque control usually does not stay isolated. It often coexists with weak payment and access control elsewhere.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Cheque processes may appear old-fashioned, but they can still cause serious loss when controls are weak. Infrequent processes are often the easiest to forget and therefore the easiest to abuse. Strong control over cheque stock protects the organization’s payment environment and supports a culture in which every payment method is governed, even the ones used less often. That consistency matters because cash control should not depend on the payment instrument’s popularity. The organization should treat every payment path as a risk path until proven otherwise.

Send to the team:
- “Please treat all cheque stock as controlled inventory and utilize positive pay services where available.”
Key point:
- Physical payment instruments must be tracked, stored, and reconciled with strict discipline.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- Association for Financial Professionals: Treasury Management
11. Q: What controls apply to bank transfers and payment instructions?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Bank transfers should always begin with a complete payment instruction supported by evidence such as invoices, contracts, payroll schedules, purchase approvals, or reimbursement documents. The instruction should clearly identify the beneficiary, the amount, the currency, the source of funds, the purpose of payment, and the requested date. A payment package is not complete unless it explains why the transfer is legitimate. The more money that is moving, the more important it is that the instruction be clear, supportable, and traceable. That is what distinguishes controlled disbursement from casual movement of cash. A valid transfer should always be explainable after the fact without reconstruction.

Governance and Control Framework
Batch payments require special discipline. The total should be reviewed, the file version should be controlled, and the approval trail should show who reviewed, who authorized, and who released the batch. If the organization uses uploaded files, the file version should be preserved so there is no ambiguity about what was approved. Emergency payments may be necessary occasionally, but urgency should never remove documentation, review, or later confirmation. The process should define what counts as urgent, who may approve an exception, and what follow-up evidence is required. Speed does not replace control; it just makes control more important. In a strong system, every urgent case leaves an obvious trail.

Operational Execution and Examples
The payment workflow should distinguish between preparation and release. A person may enter the payment, but another must verify and release it. If beneficiary details change, the change should be separately confirmed using a trusted channel rather than accepted at face value. This is especially important because payment instruction fraud often uses urgency and impersonation. A strong workflow also reduces honest mistakes: wrong bank details, wrong amount, wrong date, wrong currency, or wrong account are all easier to catch when the approval process is explicit and layered. Payment instructions should therefore be treated as formal financial records, not as casual administrative messages. The same care used for the accounting record should be used for the transfer instruction itself.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
Electronic transmission to the bank should be secure, using appropriate protocols such as SFTP, AS2, digital signatures, or bank-approved secure portals. Payment data should reconcile to the ERP and to the bank confirmation. Any discrepancy should be investigated and resolved promptly. The organization should also monitor for unusual activity such as payments to new beneficiaries, unusual jurisdictions, or unusually large amounts. These are not only control issues but also potential fraud indicators. A mature process combines prevention, detection, and later reconciliation so that the payment chain is hard to exploit and easy to review. The evidence trail should always make the payment defensible.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Controlled payment instructions make cash outflow more predictable and therefore easier to plan. They also protect the organization’s reputation because beneficiaries and regulators can see that payments are made only when properly authorized. In strategic terms, the payment process is one of the most visible demonstrations of control maturity. If the organization can handle transfers carefully, it is more likely to be trusted with larger or more sensitive cash responsibilities. Good payment control therefore supports both operational confidence and external trust.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure every bank transfer is supported by complete documentation and follows the approved authority matrix.”
Key point:
- Payment instructions must be secure, documented, and reconciled to prevent unauthorized disbursements.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- Association for Financial Professionals: Treasury Management
12. Q: How should online banking and cybersecurity risks be managed?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Online banking increases speed and visibility, but it also increases cyber risk. Strong online banking control depends on secure authentication, unique user access, role-based permissions, separated approval steps, and protected devices. The goal is to make it hard for an unauthorized person to get in, hard for a legitimate user to misuse access, and easy to see what happened after the fact. Security is therefore not separate from banking controls. It is part of them. A secure banking environment must assume that attackers will exploit convenience whenever possible.

Governance and Control Framework
The organization should use strong passwords, multi-factor authentication, secure tokens, and least-privilege access. The device used for banking should be protected from malware and unauthorized software. Authentication devices should be treated as sensitive control items. If compromise is suspected, access should be changed immediately and the bank notified. The organization should also maintain fallback procedures for outages, expired credentials, or system downtime. Those procedures must still require approval and must not become informal shortcuts. The fact that digital banking is faster does not make it safer by itself. In many ways, it simply makes bad decisions move faster too.

Operational Execution and Examples
Cyber threats such as phishing and Business Email Compromise are especially dangerous because they exploit trust and routine. A fraudster may impersonate an executive, a vendor, or a bank representative to request an urgent payment or beneficiary change. For that reason, any change to bank details should be verified through an independent trusted channel, such as a known phone number already on file. The organization should also run phishing simulations and user awareness training so that staff can recognize impersonation attempts. Security controls are strongest when technology and behavior are aligned. Without staff discipline, even a strong platform can be undermined.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The online banking environment should be reviewed periodically by IT and finance together. User logs, failed logins, unusual access times, and unusual device patterns should be reviewed. Backup and recovery processes should be tested. Emergency access should be tightly controlled and later reviewed. Auditors often focus on this area because it combines financial loss risk with cyber risk. A weak login structure can expose the entire cash environment, even if all other financial controls appear strong. Cybersecurity and finance controls therefore need to be designed as one system, not two separate ones. A bank platform without cyber discipline is not truly controlled.

Strategic Management and Reporting
A digital platform is only as secure as the controls around it. Convenience does not replace governance. By integrating cyber security into banking control, the organization protects assets, maintains continuity, and preserves trust in electronic payment operations. In practical terms, online banking should be treated as a high-value operational system with formal governance, not as a convenience tool with casual oversight. Strong cybersecurity is therefore part of sound treasury management.

Send to the team:
- “Please enforce multi-factor authentication and strict verification procedures for all online banking activities.”
Key point:
- Online banking security requires combining prevention, detection, and recovery within the control framework.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
13. Q: What is the process for obtaining and reconciling bank statements?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Bank statements should be obtained regularly, at least monthly, and more frequently where transaction volume or risk is high. They should be complete, retained, and filed whether received on paper or electronically. Missing or delayed statements are not trivial administrative issues. They can indicate access problems, operational weakness, or abnormal account activity. The statement is the independent record that the organization uses to confirm what the bank says happened, so it must be available on time and in full. A missing statement is therefore a missing control input.

Governance and Control Framework
Reconciliation compares the bank balance with the ledger or cashbook balance as of the same date. Differences must be explained, not merely listed. Common reconciling items include outstanding cheques, deposits in transit, bank charges, interest, transfers in process, and foreign exchange timing differences. A good reconciliation does not merely show a number; it explains why the number is different and what is being done to resolve the difference. The process should identify whether each item is temporary, procedural, or a sign of error. Reconciliation therefore serves both as proof and as diagnosis.

Operational Execution and Examples
Reconciliations should be prepared by one person and reviewed independently by another. The reviewer should inspect aged items, challenge repeated differences, and confirm that old items are not being rolled forward without explanation. If a reconciliation remains open too long, the organization should ask whether the bank record, the ledger, or the process itself contains a problem. Reconciliations should not be treated as a back-office task to be filed and forgotten. They are one of the strongest diagnostic tools the finance team has for understanding whether the cash record is actually true. A reconciliation that is not understood is not really complete.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The reconciliation process should be integrated with the general ledger and subledgers. Automated journal entries for bank charges or interest may reduce manual error if controlled properly. Cut-off procedures should ensure transactions are recorded in the correct accounting period. Auditors will look for timely, fully explained reconciliations and evidence that differences are resolved rather than hidden. If the process is weak, the organization may still produce a report, but the report will not be reliable enough for assurance or decision-making. Good reconciliation discipline is therefore essential to the integrity of the cash record. It is one of the clearest signs that the cash system is under control.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Consistent reconciliation helps management know the true cash position. That matters for payroll, vendor payments, grant commitments, and liquidity planning. The monthly reconciliation is one of the main ways the finance function proves that the ledger and the bank agree, or explains precisely why they do not yet agree. That makes reconciliation both a control mechanism and a strategic information process. The organization should therefore regard reconciliation as essential operational maintenance rather than as a purely accounting formality.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure all bank reconciliations are prepared and independently reviewed by the month-end close deadline.”
Key point:
- Reconciliation is a diagnostic tool that keeps the ledger connected to reality.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- PCAOB: Auditing Standards on Confirmations and Cash
14. Q: How should reconciling items and ageing be managed?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Reconciling items should be aged, monitored, and resolved. A current-month difference may simply reflect timing. An item carried into the next month deserves explanation. A balance that persists for several months needs escalation because it may reflect missing transactions, duplicate postings, unpresented instruments, bank error, or process failure. Ageing matters because unresolved items tend not to improve on their own. They either resolve through active follow-up or remain as evidence of a weak process. Aged items are therefore both a financial and a control signal.

Governance and Control Framework
Every reconciling item should have an owner, a reason, and a target clearance date. Broad explanations such as “timing difference” are not enough once the item is old. The organization should know whether the item is a cheque not yet presented, a deposit in transit, a bank adjustment, a disputed charge, or a ledger error. The more specific the explanation, the easier it is to decide whether the item is temporary or requires correction. A good ageing schedule makes it impossible for unresolved items to hide inside a large total without someone noticing. The ageing report should therefore be reviewed as part of management oversight, not just filed as a routine attachment.

Operational Execution and Examples
Old reconciling items may need reversal, write-back, reclassification, or escalation. Repeated items should be investigated for root cause rather than simply carried forward each month. If the same item keeps reappearing, the underlying process is probably broken. For example, repeated unpresented cheques may indicate weak disbursement discipline, while repeated deposits in transit may indicate posting delays or misleading cut-off practices. Ageing is therefore not just a reporting measure. It is a signal about the quality of the underlying control environment. Good follow-up should focus not only on clearing old items but on preventing recurrence.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
Persistent reconciling items can be a fraud indicator. For example, old outstanding cheques may suggest fictitious vendors, while unexplained deposits may point to other cash irregularities. Internal audit should review ageing as part of its plan. Management should ask not only how much remains unreconciled but why it has remained unreconciled. If items are old and repetitive, the organization should change the process rather than continue to tolerate the symptom. Reconciliation ageing is often one of the clearest ways to see whether the finance function is truly in control of cash. It is also one of the most practical early-warning indicators for cash integrity.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Over time, the pattern of ageing can matter more than a single month’s difference. Chronic old items suggest a process that is accommodating unresolved error instead of eliminating it. A disciplined finance team does not merely list aged items; it actively clears them and fixes the reason they occurred in the first place. That discipline strengthens the accuracy of cash reporting and makes the bank balance more credible to management and auditors alike. The organization should therefore treat ageing analysis as a control dashboard, not a passive report.

Send to the team:
- “Please assign an owner and a target clearance date to every reconciling item older than 30 days.”
Key point:
- Aging reconciling items reveal deeper process failures and potential fraud indicators.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- PCAOB: Auditing Standards on Confirmations and Cash
15. Q: How should suspense accounts and unresolved differences be handled?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Suspense accounts should be used only temporarily and for a specific purpose: holding items that cannot yet be classified while investigation or coding is completed. They are not a substitute for control and must never become a permanent parking place for unexplained balances. If suspense is used, the policy should define who may post to it, what evidence is required, how long items may remain there, and who is responsible for follow-up. A suspense account that is allowed to grow is usually a sign that the organization is accepting uncertainty instead of resolving it. The temporary nature of suspense should always be visible in practice.

Governance and Control Framework
Every suspense item should have a clear investigation path. The organization should know whether the issue is a bank posting error, a missing document, a coding mistake, or a possibly unauthorized transaction. If the amount is material or unusual, management should be informed promptly instead of waiting for month-end repeats. Suspense should reduce uncertainty temporarily, not conceal it indefinitely. A separate suspense register is a useful tool because it records the date, amount, source, reason, status, and responsible owner for each item. That makes it easier to see whether the same types of items recur and whether the process itself needs to change. Good suspense governance is therefore a control over ambiguity.

Operational Execution and Examples
Suspense accounts are sometimes needed when a payment or receipt arrives before enough information is available for final coding. That is acceptable only if the item is investigated promptly and reclassified once the missing information is found. If the same suspense category is used repeatedly for the same type of issue, the root cause should be fixed. For example, if unidentified bank receipts appear regularly, the receipt process may be too weak. If supplier invoices repeatedly land in suspense, procurement or documentation controls may need correction. The goal is not merely to move the balance out of sight but to eliminate the reason it entered suspense in the first place. Suspense should be a bridge to resolution, not a hiding place.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
Suspense accounts can be a significant risk factor for misstatement and fraud because they hold unresolved transactions. They should therefore be monitored closely and reviewed regularly. Internal audit should include them in its plan. Management should also require aging analysis and evidence of clearance. The longer a suspense item remains open, the more likely it is that the item is not temporary at all but a symptom of a control weakness. A disciplined suspense process reduces the chance that unresolved items will distort the financial statements or hide a more serious issue. Suspense becomes acceptable only when it is actively managed and promptly cleared.

Strategic Management and Reporting
A healthy control environment uses suspense sparingly, investigates quickly, and closes with evidence rather than habit. If suspense becomes routine, the organization is no longer fully in control of its financial classification process. By enforcing strict suspense management, leadership protects the integrity of the ledger and strengthens the credibility of its reporting. Suspense should be a temporary bridge, not a permanent structure. The finance team should therefore view suspense balances as issues to solve, not as balances to tolerate.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure all suspense items are investigated and cleared within 30 days of posting.”
Key point:
- Suspense accounts are for temporary clarity, not permanent concealment of unresolved risk.
Official links:
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
- IFAC: Knowledge Gateway
16. Q: What are the treasury and cash management control objectives?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Treasury and cash management exist to ensure that cash is available where it is needed, when it is needed, in the right amount, and under proper control. The objective is not only to keep money safe, but also to use it efficiently so that idle balances do not accumulate unnecessarily while operating accounts remain underfunded. In practical terms, treasury control answers four basic questions: how much cash exists, where it is held, how quickly it can be accessed, and what the minimum working balance should be. Once an organization holds multiple bank accounts, multiple currencies, or material balances, treasury becomes a central control function rather than a background administrative task. It governs the relationship between liquidity, operational continuity, and financial discipline.

Governance and Control Framework
Cash management should be based on real balances, actual settlement timing, and documented authority rather than habit or assumption. Daily or weekly visibility over all accounts allows treasury to identify surplus funds, funding gaps, float positions, and unusual movements. The function should also monitor bank charges, transfer costs, and the expense of maintaining separate account structures. Good treasury governance therefore looks beyond the face value of a balance and asks what that balance really means in operational terms. A large nominal balance may still be unavailable because it is restricted, tied up in settlement, or held in the wrong jurisdiction. A small balance may be sufficient if it is where the organization can actually use it. Treasury control is therefore as much about accessibility as about quantity.

Operational Execution and Examples
Where sweep arrangements, cash pooling, or automatic transfers are used, those mechanisms must be documented, understood, and reconciled. Automation can improve efficiency, but it also introduces risk if no one understands the rules that trigger a movement of funds. A sweep may prevent idle balances from accumulating, but it may also create donor-compliance problems, tax issues, or overdrafts if the cash flow model is wrong. Treasury must therefore review both the design and the effect of each arrangement. If an account is used for several purposes, the team should know exactly how funds move between them and who has approved that structure. A well-managed treasury file should make each movement explainable without guesswork.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
Treasury arrangements can have tax and regulatory implications, particularly when money moves across jurisdictions. Cash pooling and sweep structures may raise transfer pricing questions, withholding tax issues, or bank reporting consequences, so treasury should not design these arrangements in isolation from tax and legal specialists. Each material arrangement should be supported by documentation showing purpose, approval, and reconciliation. A well-controlled treasury file should allow an auditor to see not just that the money moved, but why it moved and whether the movement was permitted under policy. That makes the function both operationally useful and defensible. Treasury should also maintain evidence of all reviews, approvals, and balance checks so that the control environment is visible over time rather than inferred after the fact.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Treasury control is both a day-to-day discipline and a strategic capability. It informs payment timing, funding prioritization, borrowing decisions, and account rationalization. A strong treasury function does not wait for shortages to appear before acting. It uses visibility and forecasting to prevent avoidable pressure, preserve liquidity, and support financial stability. When treasury is well run, the organization can reduce banking inefficiency, maintain availability of funds, and manage risk without sacrificing speed or flexibility. In this sense, treasury is not just the place where cash is stored. It is the place where financial resilience is actively managed.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure all cash pooling and sweep arrangements are documented, understood, and regularly reconciled.”
Key point:
- Treasury management must balance cash efficiency with strict visibility and governance.
Official links:
- Association for Financial Professionals: Treasury Management
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
- IFAC: Knowledge Gateway
17. Q: How are foreign currency accounts and exchange risks controlled?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Foreign currency accounts should exist only where there is a real business need, such as international payments, donor requirements, or project operations in another currency. Every additional currency account adds complexity, monitoring effort, and exposure to exchange movement. The control objective is to ensure that the organization understands not only the nominal balance in the account, but also the value of that balance in reporting currency and the operational purpose it serves. A foreign currency account should therefore be treated as a managed exposure, not as a casual convenience. The presence of multiple currencies means the organization must think in both functional and transactional terms at once.

Governance and Control Framework
The organization should know whether each foreign currency balance is intended for immediate use, periodic disbursement, or long-term holding. It should also understand how exchange gains and losses arise through remeasurement, conversion rates, and timing differences. Those movements should be recorded correctly and not confused with posting errors. The account structure should support clear separation between transaction currency and functional or reporting currency so that the finance team can distinguish normal exchange effects from genuine control breakdowns. That distinction is essential in any multi-currency environment because a correct balance in local currency can still be a misleading balance in reporting currency. Monitoring must therefore operate at both levels.

Operational Execution and Examples
Reconciliation of foreign currency accounts requires additional care because the bank statement, general ledger, and local operational records may each reflect different exchange values. A receipt in one currency may settle at one rate, be converted at another, and be reported at a third. Differences caused by bank charges, market movement, or timing should be explained explicitly. The finance team should not leave those differences sitting in suspense or class them as unexplained errors if they are in fact ordinary currency effects. Good documentation should show what happened, when it happened, and which rate was used at each stage. The control question is not only whether the cash is present, but whether the valuation is accurate and the movement is traceable.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The organization must consider the liquidity, capital-control, and regulatory implications of each foreign currency account. In some jurisdictions, funds may be difficult to repatriate or may be subject to restrictions on conversion or transfer. The organization should therefore keep a close watch on access risk as well as exchange risk. Where balances are material, treasury should track expected use and revaluation impact regularly, ensuring that the account remains aligned with actual operational needs. A properly controlled foreign currency account is transparent, justified, and periodically reviewed. It should never become a forgotten balance that fluctuates without explanation.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Foreign currency accounts can be useful, but only if the organization has clear visibility over exchange exposure and settlement timing. A good control framework protects the organization from hidden losses, misleading balance figures, and avoidable conversion costs. By keeping foreign currency accounts justified, monitored, and reconciled, the organization improves both financial reporting quality and liquidity discipline. It also reduces the chance that a currency issue will only be noticed after it has affected budgets or donor reports.

Send to the team:
- “Please ensure all foreign currency accounts are justified by business need and actively monitored for exchange exposure.”
Key point:
- Foreign currency accounts require dual-currency reconciliation and active exchange risk management.
Official links:
- Association for Financial Professionals: Treasury Management
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
- IFAC: Knowledge Gateway
18. Q: How should bank charges, interest, and fees be monitored?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Bank charges, interest, and fees should be reviewed every period because small recurring items can conceal larger issues. The control objective is to ensure that every debit and credit from the bank is expected, properly authorized, and correctly classified. A charge that appears ordinary may still be wrong, duplicated, excessive, or inconsistent with the agreed tariff. Interest income and interest expense should also be tracked carefully because they often reveal both treasury performance and account configuration quality. The discipline required here is simple: every recurring bank movement should be explainable. If it is not immediately clear why the bank charged or credited something, the team should ask before the item becomes accepted as normal.

Governance and Control Framework
The finance team should compare bank debits to the approved fee schedule and investigate anything unfamiliar. A fee increase may reflect legitimate account activity, but it may also indicate that the bank changed pricing, applied the wrong tariff, or misclassified the account type. Interest should be reconciled against the bank’s calculation and the organization’s own expected balance profile. If the account is meant to earn interest, that income should appear in the ledger correctly and on time. If the account incurs overdraft or facility charges, those should be monitored against the agreed terms and reviewed for adequacy of use. A well-governed account should show predictable, explainable pricing behavior.

Operational Execution and Examples
Charge review becomes especially important where many small items are processed through the same account. A low-value fee may not look material in isolation, but repeated over many months it can become a significant cost. Fee monitoring also helps identify automation or configuration problems. For example, if a bank begins charging transaction fees for a service that should be free, the issue may be a change in account status rather than a random error. Similarly, if interest is lower than expected, the cause may be timing, balance volatility, or a missed deposit. The team should not assume that small amounts do not matter. Treasury control is often strongest when it notices the small things early enough to stop them from becoming a recurring pattern.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
Periodic review of bank charges is a strong control signal because it proves the organization is actively testing the bank relationship rather than passively accepting what appears on the statement. The review should be documented, signed off, and linked to any follow-up on anomalies. If a charge is found to be wrong, the organization should challenge it promptly and keep evidence of the correction. This creates an audit trail showing that the bank account is under management and not simply under observation. A reliable review process can also reveal whether some accounts are unnecessarily expensive and should be closed or renegotiated. The control is therefore both corrective and strategic.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Fee monitoring is not only about cost containment. It is also about governance quality. An organization that knows its banking costs can make better decisions about account structure, transaction methods, and treasury design. It can reduce avoidable charges, improve net returns on cash, and demonstrate stronger stewardship. The more disciplined the charge review, the more confidence leadership can have in the accuracy of banking costs and interest reporting. A strong review routine also helps the organization decide whether a particular account remains worth keeping.

Send to the team:
- “Please review all bank charges and fees monthly against the agreed tariff schedule and investigate any variances.”
Key point:
- Routine bank fees must be monitored as they can hide configuration errors and accumulate into material costs.
Official links:
- Association for Financial Professionals: Treasury Management
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
- Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA)
19. Q: How are loans, overdrafts, and contingent facilities controlled?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
Loans, overdrafts, guarantees, standby letters of credit, and similar banking facilities should be managed as controlled obligations rather than as ordinary extensions of the operating account. Even if no cash has yet moved, these instruments can create real financial exposure, fee obligations, or future claims. The control objective is to know the size, purpose, limit, expiry date, and cost of each facility and to keep that information current. If a facility is no longer required, it should be closed formally instead of being left dormant and forgotten. The organization should treat borrowing and contingent support as part of its risk structure, not as a convenience feature of the bank relationship.

Governance and Control Framework
The organization should keep a register of all borrowing and contingent banking arrangements, including approval evidence and renewal conditions. Utilization should be compared to approved need, not to informal expectation. A facility that exists “just in case” may still require disclosure, monitoring, and periodic review. Where the instrument is contingent rather than fully drawn, finance should still know whether it affects commitments, contingent liabilities, or cash planning. The account control framework must therefore extend beyond visible balances to include potential obligations and off-balance-sheet exposures. The register should show who owns the facility and who is responsible for monitoring renewal and compliance dates.

Operational Execution and Examples
Overdrafts can distort liquidity reporting if the team does not track them carefully. They may also increase the cost of capital or signal operational stress if used repeatedly. Guarantees or letters of credit may affect contract performance or procurement arrangements even without immediate cash movement. Each arrangement should be documented and understood by treasury, finance, and, where necessary, legal or tax specialists. A clean facility register allows management to see which instruments support operations and which are no longer aligned with actual needs. If the team cannot explain why the facility still exists, it probably needs review or closure.

Internal Controls and Audit Readiness
The organization must consider the implications of these facilities for credit standing, disclosure, and lender compliance. If borrowing terms are breached, the impact may be greater than the immediate cash effect because it can affect covenant status or future financing access. The team should therefore review facility terms regularly and ensure all reported balances and commitments are complete. This is especially important when facilities are shared across entities or jurisdictions. Every contingent exposure should have an owner, a purpose, and a documented monitoring routine. A facility that is visible, tracked, and periodically revalidated is far safer than one that sits quietly in the background.

Strategic Management and Reporting
Proper control of loans and contingent facilities helps the organization avoid hidden liabilities and preserve flexibility. By keeping the register current, management can make better decisions about borrowing, liquidity, and capital structure. The result is stronger financial discipline and less exposure to surprise obligations. A rigorous facility control process also supports stronger lender relationships because the organization can respond clearly to questions about utilization, purpose, and compliance.

Send to the team:
- “Please maintain a comprehensive register of all banking facilities, including limits, expiry dates, and utilization.”
Key point:
- Contingent facilities create real obligations and exposures that must be tracked and disclosed.
Official links:
- Association for Financial Professionals: Treasury Management
- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)
- COSO Internal Control - Integrated Framework
20. Q: What controls apply to merchant accounts and payment gateways?
A: Show answer
Core Principle and Definition
